runs.eval_name: Generate 5 YouTube title variations for dollar store giveaway video

expectations.text: Produces exactly 5 title variations
At least one title uses a survival/extreme verb (Survived, Bought Everything, Emptied)
At least one title uses a dollar amount or scale indicator
Titles are ranked 1-5 by predicted CTR with reasoning for the ranking
The top-ranked title is more extreme/intriguing than the weakest one

runs.eval_name: Compare CTR of homemade pasta vs world's longest pasta and suggest thumbnails

expectations.text: Correctly identifies B as higher CTR with clear reasoning
Explains the role of 'World's Longest' as a scale/superlative trigger
Thumbnail concept for B emphasizes scale (person dwarfed by pasta, measurement visible)
Notes that thumbnail A has no obvious visual hook
References the principle that extremity outperforms neutral framing

runs.eval_name: Diagnose CTR problem for homeless people video and rewrite title and thumbnail

expectations.text: Correctly diagnoses the title as generic/low-extremity
Identifies the thumbnail as showing a generic conversation scene with no hook
Rewritten title adds scale, number, or outcome (e.g., 'I Gave 100 Homeless People...', '$1,000,000 to Homeless Families')
Rewritten thumbnail concept shows transformation, scale, or emotional peak moment
Explicitly notes that the rewrite must not misrepresent the content (expectations must be met)

notes: Both with_skill and without_skill configurations achieved a perfect 1.0 pass rate across all 3 evals (15/15 expectations passed each).

The skill produced measurably richer, more structured responses: with_skill responses included explicit skill-framework terminology (extremity rule, open loop, thumbnail contract, production implications), while without_skill responses covered the same high-level points but with less systematic structure.

The current eval expectations appear insufficient to discriminate between skill-guided and baseline performance. All 5 core CTR concepts tested (extremity, scale, visual hook, superlative triggers, accuracy caveat) are within reach of a capable baseline model without skill guidance.

Qualitative differences observed: with_skill responses (1) explicitly applied named frameworks from the skill (e.g., 'survival format', 'open loop principle', 'thumbnail/title contract'), (2) included production implications, (3) provided more granular CTR reasoning tied to the skill's checklist. These differences are not captured by the current expectations.

Recommendation: Add expectations that check for skill-specific constructs (e.g., 'Response cites production implications', 'Response uses the open loop principle by name', 'Response provides a CTR self-test checklist') to make the evals more discriminating.
